text: The robotic arm holds the lung up and out of view while the surgeon uses bipolar cautery to detach the subcarinal lymph node from the bronchus. The video then shows dissection of the large bronchial artery associated with the number seven lymph node, followed by pleura dissection off the esophagus to resect the node completely. Later, the sump lymph node or number eleven node is identified, with the right upper lobe bronchus visible at the top of the screen and the bronchus intermedius at the bottom. The assistant uses a suction device to hold down the bronchus intermedius, allowing dissection of the number eleven node from the pulmonary artery between the A6 and A2 branches.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative bronchoscopy on a patient with a large tumor in the right lower lobe. Using Firefly imaging, the bronchus and airway are visualized without ICG. Scissors are used to open the airway, ensuring the inspired PaO2 is less than thirty before using monopolar or bipolar energy. The bronchus intermedius is opened distally to inspect the tumor and achieve negative margins. A bipolar device is used to dissect the bronchus, aiming to preserve the middle lobe during the robotic operation.

At the two minute and thirty-eight second mark, the surgeon carefully removes a large interdigitating number ten lymph node from the pulmonary artery near the upper lobe. The assistant retracts the superior segment of the right lower lobe downward. A sponge temporarily obstructs the view but is promptly moved. The surgeon gently pushes the pulmonary artery aside to facilitate the removal of the lymph node, enhancing mobility of the right upper lobe bronchus for the upcoming sleeve procedure. By the three minute and twenty-three second mark, the N-one node is dissected off the bronchus intermedius, fully freeing the bronchus for resection.

The surgeon navigates around the arterial branches of the right lower lobe, identifying the anterior basilar branches and the A-six superior segment. The robotic stapler is introduced to divide the right lower lobe pulmonary artery, providing a clear view of the bronchus to the lower lobe. The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to separate the bronchus intermedius from the right middle lobe, ensuring clear margins. Scissors are then used to send tissue for frozen section analysis.

The surgeon resects the number ten lymph nodes from the right main stem bronchus, freeing them from the azygos vein. The surgical field includes visible anatomical structures such as the trachea, pericardium, innominate vein, innominate artery, and vagus nerve. The margins are confirmed negative, and the surgeon begins the anastomosis using a RB-I-III-O needle with Stratafix sutures, starting at the five or six o'clock position on the bronchus intermedius and continuing the suturing process on the middle lobe.

The surgeon uses a Stratafix suture with an eyelet to perform bronchial anastomosis, passing the needle through the eyelet without tying a knot. The barbed suture is tightened to prevent loosening. The surgeon continues suturing clockwise, starting outside to in on the bronchus intermedius and inside to out on the middle lobe bronchus, ensuring precise alignment of the bronchi.

The surgeon uses a continuous suture technique with Stratafix, moving in an anti-clockwise direction along the cartilaginous portion of the bronchus. The DeBakey forceps are used to handle the Stratafix, avoiding the needle driver to prevent snapping the suture. The procedure transitions to the membranous portion of the bronchus, where generous suture bites are taken. The anastomosis is completed with a single knot, ensuring no air leaks, as confirmed by the absence of bubbling.
